i The extraordinarily extensive and valuable library’’—to quote
| the words of the auctioneer—formed by Dr. John Murphy, Bishop of
| Cork, has been frequé.mtly, though inadequately, referred to in these
"pages. As it was the largest collection ever formed in Ireland by a
| private individual, a fuller description. than any attempted before may
| be welcome, a description only recently rendered possible by the fact
 that the thousands of auctioneers’ catalogues, fully priced, that have
so long lain perdu in the British Museum have been catalogued, and
'a'.re now readily accessible to the searcher. On the death of the 1llus-
| trious prelate, the books and MSS., not otherwise disposed of in his
L will, were ‘‘ consigned to London by order of the administrators,” and
'.e'ﬁ-tl'u-s_lted for sale to Sotheby’s, then, as now, in Wellington Stree-t,‘
‘_Stra.nd. Such was the enormous number of books, covering practically
L every subject under _t.'he sun—a real bibliomaniac’s collection—that the
 auctioneers spread it over a whole year! The sale of the first portion
{ commenced on Wednesday, 3rd November, 1847, and continued for
ten days. The catalogue of this portion alone runs to 143 octavo
;I'?age«s , and enumerates over three thousand entries of miscellaneous
?_;:books, some of the lots containing seventeen and eighteen volumes.
‘The total amount realized was £1,312 9s. The sale of the second
i.bqrbion has already heen described by Mr. James Coleman (I.B.L.,
_f':Yol. II., p. 19), and only requires the addendum that it commenced
on 13th December, 1847, and realized £1,003 19s. 6d. The catalogue
of the third portion (pp. 102) contained 1,912 lots, ranging from 1 to
32 vols., each. It commenced on 2nd Iebruary, 1848, continued for
'six days, and realized £595 11s. 6d. The disposal of the fourth por-
Efon commenced on 1st May, 1848, and lasted nine days. This cata-
IOg‘ue runs to 172 pages, and the lots total 92,872. In this were In-

BALLINASLOE PrixTING. The name of Thomas rench occurs 1 oeh
Slater’s Directory of 1846 as that of the only printer in Ballinasloe, Dr

Apparently he opened his printing office here in 1838, as he has an

advertisement in the ‘ Leinster Express’’ of the 29th Dacember of (
ﬁhat year, which announces ‘‘that he has lately opened the above d?s’ol
Establishment.”’ ~ Neither Cotton nor Power mention Ballinaslos uon
French did not introduce the printing press here, however, as M I b
Dix gives ‘‘ 1828’ as the earliest known date for Ballinasloe printing 101?

29th, 1838, which has just come into my possession, I find that “Jon |
Lahee’’ was at that’time printing in Carlow. He is mentioned inh Lon
Slater’s Directory of 1846, but not in Pigot’s, of 1824, nor in a l:ist;" s
of Carlow printing by Mr. E. R. McC. Dix, which ends with t‘he;?. o
year 1825. It may be added that this issue has half a column off o

‘“ Tmportant L.aw Works relating to Ireland, lately published bjif Bal
Milliken and Son, Grafton-street, Dublin’’; and a notice appears 1n C
it, as well as an advertisement, of the first number of the ‘‘ Dublin
Medical Press,”’” to be published on the Qth' of the following January. }:

Naas PrintiNg. In the ““ Leinster Express’’ of December 29, 1838, B ?;E

description of Letter Press Printing, are executed at this Bstablish- -
ment . . . on moderate terms, and the shortest pO&S].blﬁ notice.” f

The paper is stated at the end of the fourth page to be ‘ Printed and|
Published at the Offices in Maryborough and Naas, by H. W. Taibot. ”'
In Slater’s Directory (1846), however, there is no mention of *; ~ th

in a separate notice under Naas, the newspaper itself being also not.ed n
under hoth towns. Cotton does not mention Naas, nor does Power,| Pt

lowing Slater, has ‘“1846°’ as the year of the earliest printing in{
Naas which (whatever may be the origin of Cotton’s statement undert

GREENISLAND PRINTING. The existence of a press in this northern
district a few miles from Belfast, and now an important railway junc-
tion, has never been noted before, and deserves putting on record.
I have recently obtained a well-printed little volume, 5% x 3%, pp.
108, entitled ‘‘ The Patriotic Sailor, or Adventures of a Naval Life,”
published by Archer and Sons, Uastle Place, Belfast, 1841. The colo-
phon runs, ‘‘Mairs, Printer, Greenisland.”” Thomas Mairs flourished
as a printer in Belfast from 1810, and did some excellent work for
London, as well as local, publishers. It may be that on his retire-

~ ment from business he kept a small press to occupy his leisure hours,

or he may have been succeeded by a son. DPerhaps some of your

northern readers could say which, and, as when first T recorded the

Ballyclare Press (Vol. V., p. 129), add some additional information.
Chatham. STOUPPE McCANCE, A.S.C.

Newry PrinTiNG. I have recemntly obtained a copy of ‘‘ The Orange
Bard, or Loyal Songster.”” Newry: Printed by John Nelson, Mill St.,
1798. 12mo., pp. 72. It is worthy of note as being ‘‘the first collec-
tion ever published in this part of the Kingdom,” and because Mr.
Philip Crossle in his exhaustive account of the Newry printers could
trace nothing to Nelson’s press previous to 1804. '

and John Power, were unable to fix a date, even approximately, for
the introduction of printing into this town. The earliest i1mprint
known to Mr. Dix was dated 1821. T have recently acquired a speci-
men antedating this by twenty years. It is entitled ‘ Female Emter-

Prize: or a Trip to Matrimony. A Comic Entertainment written I_Jy
g Andrew Hamilton, A.M. Omagh: Printed by T. Collins, 1801.”” 8vo.,

i pp. 2.

To ‘‘Notes and Queries’’ for 19th June, Mr. Esposito contrlbutes a CAA
scholarly account of one of the most ancient MSS. of Irish origh Mes
now in existence, the so-called ‘Cathach MS,” or ‘Psalter of St tha
(jol-um-ba.,’ the property of the O’Donnell family, of Newport, Co.': refe
Mayo. Believed to have originally consisted of 110 folios written i M2
single columns, at present there remains only 58 leaves, all more a a1
less decayed, many having especially suffered as a result of a process'-' We
of steeping in cold water, adopted by Sir Wm. Betham, to open om |
the fragment a century ago. What mnow remains compuseq we.
Psalms 30, 10, to 105 and 13, and the writer considers that 1t dates; V!
from the latter part of the seventh century, about a century l-a,ter‘e Sl
than St. Columba’s time. W

the most satisfactory ever issued by that flourishing association, s o
Through, the publication of two volumes last year they are mow up h
to date. During the present year the Council hope to publish t-he%“ b
third and concluding volume of Rev. J. MacErlean’s edition ofi A b
O’Bruadair’s Poems which is now going through the press. T]J.L‘!;
volume will contain the interesting historical poems relating to the* =
time and deeds of Sarsfield, and will throw new light on local a,ffalrs; ]
in and around Iimerick during and after the sieges in 1690-91. W :

Regarding the future output, Rev. L. McKenna’s edition of Thef’l ]
Contention of the Bards’ is now ready for the press. Mr. J. H =
Lloyd has sent in the MS. of his edition of the poems of Hugh Macr; |
Shane O’Bryne, of Wicklow, other offers of texts have been 1ece1ved
and volumes announced in former reports are in progress.

Dr. E. J. DinLox. That very interesting man, Dr. Emile J.
Dillon, who has been for twenty-five years a special foreign corres-
pondent to the ‘‘ Daily Telegraph,” was born sixty-two years ago in
Dublin. A writer in the ‘ Newspaper World’’ says that he has
oraduated at three European Universities, and he has studied at

probably known more of the secret history of Europe and the great

a leading part in many crises, and not a few esteem him as the
chief of living jourhalists. Dr. Dillon is a great Hebrew scholar, and
| the most intimate friend of his life was the illustrious Hebraist, Dr.
i Bicknell, the Roman Catholic Professor at Innsbruck. I once asked
Dr. Dillon which city he would prefer as his place of residence. - He
thought: a little and said, ‘I would like best to live where my friend
Dr. Bicknell was living.”’—Sir Robertson Nicoll in ‘¢ British Weekly.”
